title: NOC 73402 – Drillers and blasters - surface mining, quarrying and construction | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 73402 professionals are the specialized power behind Canada’s raw material extraction and heavy civil engineering projects. As a TEER 3 occupation, these experts operate heavy mobile drilling rigs to bore blast holes and use high-precision explosives to dislodge rock, coal, and ore. In 2026, as Canada ramps up "Critical Minerals" mining for global EV supply chains, these workers are classified as Strategic Resource Extraction Talent. For immigration, this role is a prime target for the 2026 Trade-Specific Express Entry draws and the BC PNP Skilled Worker stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level3Major Group73 – General tradesSub-Major Group734 – Operators, drillers and blastersMinor Group7340 – Operators, drillers and blastersNOC Code73402

duties: Rig Operation: Drive and operate tracked or truck-mounted rotary, air-track, or down-the-hole drilling machines to bore large-diameter blast holes.
Blast Layout: Read instructions or diagrams to lay out drill patterns and determine the precise depth and diameter of blast holes required.
Field Testing: Conduct field tests to determine the specific type and quantity of explosives needed based on rock density and terrain.
Primer Assembly: Assemble primer charges using selected detonators, fuses, and detonating cords according to strict safety regulations.
Loading Explosives: Load blast holes with explosives by hand or direct the movement of bulk explosive trucks to fill deep holes.
System Connection: Connect electrical wires or fuses into series and link them to blasting machines for controlled detonation.
Detonation Management: Clear the blast area of personnel and equipment before triggering the handle or button to detonate charges.
Foundation Drilling: Operate specialized drills equipped with augers or other attachments to bore holes for building foundations or heavy pilings.
Documentation: Maintain accurate digital logs of drilling depths, explosive quantities used, and blast results for safety audits.
Safety Oversight: Direct other workers in the assembly and loading of charges and ensure all provincial blasting regulations are strictly followed.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Experience: Experience as a Heavy Equipment Operator (NOC 73400) is often required for drillers; experience as a Blaster Helper is required for blasters.
Licensing (Mandatory): A Provincial Blasting License is mandatory in all provinces to handle and detonate explosives.
Certification: Trade certification for blasters is compulsory in Nova Scotia and available but voluntary in other provinces.
On-the-Job Training: Extensive specialized training (often 6–12 months) is provided by mining and construction employers.
Physical Fitness: The ability to work in remote, noisy, and hazardous outdoor environments is essential.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, specialized FIFO Blasters in Northern Canada often earn annual salaries between $95,000 and $135,000, with some senior blasters exceeding $150,000 with bonuses.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the shift toward "Green Construction" and the extraction of copper and lithium will drive a steady stream of job openings. While automation is being integrated into drilling, the complex safety and environmental judgment required for blasting ensures that human blasters will remain a critical, high-demand requirement through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The demand for drillers and blasters in 2026 is Stable to Strong, driven by massive mining expansions in Northern Ontario, British Columbia, and the Atlantic provinces. According to March 2026 Job Bank data, the outlook is "Moderate" in Ontario but significantly higher in B.C. and Newfoundland, where new open-pit mining projects are in development. The industry is facing a specific labor shortage for blasters certified in Electronic Detonation Systems. Approximately 95% of the workforce is male, and nearly all roles are full-time with significant overtime opportunities.
High-Stakes Technology: Work with the latest autonomous drilling rigs, digital blast-modeling software, and safe, high-efficiency explosive compounds.
Exceptional Pay: This is one of the highest-paying non-managerial trades in Canada, particularly for those working in fly-in/fly-out (FIFO) mining camps.
Global Safety Standards: Canada maintains some of the world's most rigorous blasting safety protocols, providing you with world-class training and credentials.
Strategic PR Pathways: This NOC is a priority for 2026 Federal and Provincial trade draws, allowing skilled operators to secure PR with lower CRS scores compared to general pool candidates.
pathways: Express Entry (Trade Occupations Category): This NOC is a high priority for 2026 category-based selection draws with lower CRS requirements.
Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP): A reliable route for operators with 2 years of experience and a valid job offer or certificate of qualification.
BC PNP Skilled Worker: Priority for experienced drillers and blasters supporting the province’s massive mining and hydroelectric sectors.
Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP): Ideal for those willing to work in regional mining hubs like Sudbury or Thompson.
